retrieved_docs.title: Hypertensive Crisis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Long-term control includes lifestyle modification and strict medication adherence.

retrieved_docs.title: Renovascular Hypertension -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Long term history of smoking

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation and Management of Perioperative Hypertension -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients with HTN report their diagnosis when presenting for a preoperative visit. Still, in a study of patients manifesting hypertensive crises, more than a third were unaware they had HTN. [13] Therefore, the importance of measuring BP at the preoperative visit cannot be overemphasized. Furthermore, it should be measured in both arms. Despite published guidelines for BP measurement and a review of BP measurement for anesthesiologists, inaccurate BP measurement is common. BP should be measured in a quiet room, with the patient sitting. [14] [15] Undersized cuffs result in erroneously elevated BP, and this is the most common cause of erroneously reported HTN. A properly sized cuff has a bladder cuff length that is 80% to 100% the circumference of the arm, while the width of the cuff should be 40% of the upper mid-arm circumference. The cuff should be centered over the brachial artery, directly on the skin. Too large a cuff usually results in an accurate reading. BP cuffs measure mean arterial pressure (MAP), with SBP and DBP determined by algorithms that vary between manufacturers. Although cuff BP is most often measured in the upper arm, it can be measured in the legs. BP is 10 to 20 mm Hg higher in the legs as compared with the arms. If BP is greater than 130/80 mm Hg but less than 160/90 mmHg, and a patient states they have white-coat HTN, it should be confirmed that white-coat HTN has been properly diagnosed with ambulatory or home BP monitoring. One to five percent of patients with white-coat hypertension converts to sustained HTN annually. The incidence is greater with older age, obesity, higher initial BP, and Blacks. White-coat HTN has been associated with slightly increased CVD risk and all-cause mortality risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthostatic Syncope (Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with autonomic dysfunction and supine hypertension, raising the head of the bed to 10 degrees at night reduces nocturnal diuresis

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertensive Crisis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: While the short-term prognosis for most patients with malignant hypertension is favorable with appropriate treatment, long-term outcomes can be compromised by frequent exacerbations. Patients who are noncompliant with therapy are at increased risk for serious complications, including stroke, vision loss, kidney damage, and adverse cardiac events. [39] Elevated cardiac troponins and renal dysfunction at presentation are associated with poor short-term outcomes. Over the long term, the risk of renal complications is influenced by the severity of proteinuria and the effectiveness of blood pressure control following recovery. [40] [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthostasis (Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacological therapy, the aim is to raise the blood pressure when the patient will stand up or will perform any activity in the upright position, avoiding side effects and the complications of increasing the blood pressure for a prolonged time. Fludrocortisone is the first-line treatment in the non-hypertensive patient. Second-line therapy includes midodrine indicated for patients with or without hypertension. [15] Other therapies include caffeine, pyridostigmine, erythropoietin, vasopressin analogues, yohimbine, among others.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertensive Urgency -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with hypertensive urgency are at increased risk for long-term morbidity and mortality. The one-year mortality for those experiencing an episode of hypertensive urgency is approximately 9%. Untreated hypertension is notorious for increasing mortality risk and is often described as a silent killer.

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation and Management of Perioperative Hypertension -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: HTN is the most common risk factor for the development of CVD and mortality. Since HTN impacts >60% of people over the age of 60 in the developed world, most studies have focused on outcomes in older patients. Still, even HTN in late adolescence is associated with increased cardiovascular causes of death in middle age, as compared with people with lower BP. [33] In other words, HTN is no longer a disease of the elderly. As in older persons, a sedentary lifestyle and obesity are contributing factors to HTN in late adolescence. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Systolic Hypertension -- Treatment / Management -- B lood Pressure Goals
retrieved_docs.content: It is suggested that a goal blood pressure of less than 130/80 mmHg is appropriate as long as the patient tolerates it.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthostatic Hypotension -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Orthostatic hypotension is characterized by a sudden drop in blood pressure that occurs upon standing from a sitting or supine position. The condition can be broadly classified as neurogenic or non-neurogenic. This condition is clinically diagnosed when systolic blood pressure decreases by at least 20 mm Hg or diastolic pressure by 10 mm Hg within 3 minutes of standing or being positioned at a 60-degree angle on a tilt table. This sudden drop in blood pressure is usually caused by autonomic reflex failure, central or peripheral nervous system lesions, cardiac dysfunction, volume depletion, or adverse medication effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthostatic Hypotension -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The management of orthostatic hypotension is determined by its underlying cause. The primary goal of the treatment is to alleviate symptoms and prevent associated morbidity rather than to normalize blood pressure. Asymptomatic patients typically do not require treatment, and some causes, such as hypovolemia or cardiac failure, may be reversible with correction of the underlying issue. Non-pharmacological interventions form the cornerstone of treatment, as pharmacological approaches raise blood pressure indiscriminately and can exacerbate supine or sitting hypertension. Non-pharmacological strategies are tailored to each patient's triggers and lifestyle, with a combination approach often proving the most effective. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthostatic Syncope (Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Dietary measures including liberal salt diet 6-10g/day, eating small frequent low carbohydrate meals a day in case of postprandial orthostatic hypotension, avoid alcohol intake

retrieved_docs.title: Orthostatic Hypotension -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms of orthostatic hypotension are triggered by postural changes and typically resolve when sitting or lying down. Symptoms may occur in the morning upon rising from bed or during the day with postural changes such as sitting to standing, lying to standing, or lying to sitting. Symptoms associated with orthostatic changes should prompt evaluation for orthostatic hypotension.

retrieved_docs.title: Systolic Hypertension -- Treatment / Management -- Elevated Blood Pressure
retrieved_docs.content: SBP 120 to 129 mm Hg and DBP less than 80 mm Hg Initiate nonpharmacologic therapy and reassess in 3 to 6 months

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperaldosteronism -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Extended duration of hypertension

retrieved_docs.title: Essential Hypertension -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management
retrieved_docs.content: Side effects are generally mild and resolve promptly upon decreasing the dosage or discontinuing the drug for short intervals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2895
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure is classified as a vascular condition, and it belongs to the broader Vascular category that also encompasses أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis), فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي (diastolic hypertension), and فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension). These entities represent related subtypes of abnormal blood pressure regulation. Elevated mean arterial pressure reflects a persistently higher average arterial pressure, while hypertensive crisis denotes an acute, severe elevation that can threaten organ function. Diastolic hypertension is characterized by consistently high diastolic pressure, and systolic hypertension is defined by consistently high systolic pressure. Recognizing that all of these disorders fall under the Vascular umbrella aids clinicians in categorizing blood‑pressure abnormalities, tailoring diagnostic evaluation, and selecting appropriate therapeutic strategies to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1936
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure, hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ), and reactive hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي) are all classified within the broader vascular category. These conditions represent distinct manifestations of abnormal vascular pressure regulation. Elevated mean arterial pressure reflects a sustained increase in the average arterial pressure throughout the cardiac cycle. Hypertensive crisis denotes an acute, severe elevation of blood pressure that can threaten organ function if not promptly treated. Primary pulmonary hypertension is a specific form of high pressure that originates in the pulmonary arteries, leading to right‑ventricular strain and potential respiratory complications. Reactive hypertension describes a secondary rise in systemic blood pressure that occurs in response to another underlying condition. Recognizing that these entities share a common vascular basis aids clinicians in assessing, differentiating, and managing patients with hypertension‑related disorders, thereby improving diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2757
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) is a primary form of high blood pressure that frequently presents with an elevated mean arterial pressure as a clinical manifestation. Hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم) is closely linked to essential hypertension, indicating that severe acute blood‑pressure elevations can arise in patients with this condition. Essential hypertension also has a recognized relationship with metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض), reflecting shared pathophysiological mechanisms and overlapping risk factors. Moreover, essential hypertension is commonly mapped to systolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانقبالي), emphasizing that the systolic component often predominates in its presentation. Understanding these interconnections helps clinicians identify the characteristic signs, potential complications, and associated comorbidities of essential hypertension, thereby improving patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is clinically associated with several conditions and findings. Patients with essential hypertension often exhibit depressive disorders, may have abnormal chest X‑ray results, and can experience tremor. In addition, essential hypertension corresponds to both diastolic hypertension and systolic hypertension, reflecting its classification into the two primary components of elevated blood pressure. Recognizing these associations and the dual nature of essential hypertension aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment and management of affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure is classified as a vascular condition that reflects an overall increase in arterial pressure. Within the broader vascular category, several specific hypertensive disorders are recognized as subtypes, including أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis), ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension), and فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension). Although these entities share the common feature of elevated blood pressure, they differ in their underlying mechanisms, affected vascular territories, and clinical manifestations. Recognizing that elevated mean arterial pressure and its vascular subtypes are interrelated helps clinicians accurately diagnose, monitor, and tailor treatment strategies for patients presenting with various forms of hypertension, thereby improving patient care and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2926
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure are both classified as vascular abnormalities, reflecting increased pressure within the circulatory system. The broader vascular category also encompasses severe pressure disorders such as أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) and ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension). Together, these entities illustrate a spectrum of vascular pressure elevations that are important for clinicians to recognize, as they can signal acute emergencies or chronic pulmonary vascular disease and guide appropriate monitoring and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1500
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure are vascular conditions that fall under the broader Vascular category. The Vascular category also includes أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis), فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي (diastolic hypertension), and فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension). These entities represent different manifestations of increased arterial pressure: mean arterial pressure reflects overall perfusion pressure, while systolic blood pressure indicates the peak pressure during cardiac contraction. Recognizing that elevated mean arterial pressure, elevated systolic blood pressure, hypertensive crisis, diastolic hypertension, and systolic hypertension are interrelated subtypes within the vascular system helps clinicians classify and manage high‑pressure states appropriately, addressing both acute emergencies such as hypertensive crisis and chronic forms like diastolic and systolic hypertension. Effective management of these elevated pressures is essential for preventing the complications associated with the spectrum of vascular hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2489
retrieved_docs.content: The comprehensive evaluation of cardiovascular function with tilt table testing, continuous ECG monitoring, and intermittent blood pressure monitoring, with or without pharmacological intervention, incorporates ضغط الدم as a key component. ضغط الدم measurements are essential for interpreting hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, guiding clinicians in assessing severity and determining appropriate treatment. ضغط الدم is directly related to ضغط الدم المنخفض, underscoring the clinical continuum from elevated to low blood pressure and the need to monitor both extremes. Malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is clinically similar to hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, indicating that patients with this form of renal arteriolar damage may present with comparable hypertensive manifestations. Recognizing these interconnections supports accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and individualized management of complex blood‑pressure disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension and hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension are both interpreted as manifestations of blood pressure (ضغط الدم) abnormalities. Blood pressure (ضغط الدم) is related to low blood pressure (ضغط الدم المنخفض), highlighting the continuum between elevated and reduced arterial pressure states. Malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is clinically similar to hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, indicating that this severe renal vascular disease shares many of the same clinical features—such as marked vascular injury and renal impairment—as complicated and secondary forms of hypertension. Understanding these interconnections helps clinicians recognize the spectrum of hypertensive disorders and their renal complications, facilitating more accurate diagnosis and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2178
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated mean arterial pressure, and elevated systolic blood pressure are all classified as Vascular conditions, indicating that each represents a form of increased arterial pressure within the vascular system. The Vascular category also encompasses أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) and ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension). Together, these entities illustrate the spectrum of vascular hypertension, ranging from chronic elevations in diastolic, systolic, and mean arterial pressures to acute severe hypertension and pulmonary‑specific hypertension, and recognizing them as children of the Vascular classification aids clinicians in organizing assessment and treatment strategies for hypertension‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1530
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated diastolic blood pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure are clinical manifestations of فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي (primary hypertension). An increase in blood pressure (زيادة ضغط الدم) is also associated with فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي. While فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي represents essential hypertension, it is conceptually linked to فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي (secondary hypertension) as part of the broader hypertension classification, distinguishing primary from secondary causes. Understanding these relationships helps clinicians recognize the hemodynamic signs of primary hypertension and differentiate it from secondary forms for appropriate evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2753
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated systolic blood pressure is classified as a vascular condition, and it belongs to the same category as several other high‑pressure disorders. Within the vascular domain, أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) represents an acute, severe elevation of blood pressure that requires immediate medical intervention. ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension) denotes a chronic increase in pressure within the pulmonary arteries that occurs without an identifiable secondary cause. فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension) describes a pattern in which only the systolic component of arterial pressure is elevated. Together, Elevated systolic blood pressure, hypertensive crisis, primary pulmonary hypertension, and systolic hypertension are all considered child entities of the broader Vascular classification, highlighting their shared relevance to vascular pathology and the importance of recognizing and managing these distinct forms of high blood pressure in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is clinically similar to hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, indicating that the severe renal vascular changes seen in malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis share comparable clinical manifestations, risk factors, and disease mechanisms with complicated primary hypertension and hypertension that arises secondary to other underlying conditions. This similarity underscores the importance of recognizing overlapping signs and symptoms when diagnosing and managing patients with advanced hypertensive organ damage and secondary causes of elevated blood pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 921
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is identified and interpreted through increased blood pressure measurements, which are central to its clinical assessment. Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis exhibits clinical features that are similar to those of essential hypertension, highlighting overlapping manifestations and the need to consider both conditions when evaluating patients with elevated blood pressure and related renal changes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis and essential hypertension are clinically similar conditions, as both involve pathological changes in small arterial vessels that lead to elevated blood pressure and related end‑organ effects. Recognizing the overlap in their clinical presentations helps clinicians differentiate primary hypertension from secondary renal vascular disease, guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation, and informs management strategies aimed at controlling blood pressure and preserving kidney function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2927
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is a form of elevated arterial pressure that is identified and interpreted through measurement of ضغط الدم (blood pressure). The concept of ضغط الدم includes the entire spectrum of arterial pressures and is directly related to ضغط الدم المنخفض (low blood pressure), illustrating the continuum from hypotension to hypertension. Recognizing how essential hypertension is defined by ضغط الدم and how ضغط الدم connects with ضغط الدم المنخفض helps clinicians assess, diagnose, and manage blood pressure disorders more effectively.
